question_structure.context: To build a civilized city, it is advocated that motor vehicles yield to pedestrians. A driver was driving at a speed of $8\mathrm{m/s}$ near an intersection when he noticed a pedestrian preparing to cross the zebra crossing. He immediately applied the brakes to yield to the pedestrian. The car underwent uniformly decelerated motion with an acceleration magnitude of $2\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^{2}$.
question_structure.sub_question_1: The speed of the car after braking for 2 seconds.
question_structure.sub_question_2: The minimum distance for a car to stop before the zebra crossing when braking
question_structure.sub_question_3: The displacement of a car braking for 3 seconds.

sub_question_1.step_1: The car is decelerating, and according to the velocity-time equation, we have: $v_{1}=v_{0}+a t_{1}=4\mathrm{m/s}$

sub_question_2.step_2: According to the velocity-time formula, the stopping time is given by $t=\frac{0-v_{0}}{a}=4\mathrm{s}$.
sub_question_2.step_3: According to the displacement-time formula, we know: $x_{1}=v_{0}t+\frac{1}{2}a t^{2}=16\mathbf{m}$

sub_question_3.step_4: During the car braking process: $x_{2}=v_{0}t_{1}+\frac{1}{2}a t_{1}^{2}=15\mathrm{m}$

step_1.physical_theorem: Velocity-time equation for uniformly accelerated motion

result_quantity.name: The speed of the car after braking for 2 seconds

step_2.physical_theorem: Velocity-time equation for uniformly accelerated motion

result_quantity.name: The stopping time of the car

step_3.physical_theorem: Displacement-time equation for uniformly accelerated motion

result_quantity.name: The minimum stopping distance of the car

step_4.physical_theorem: Displacement-time equation for uniformly accelerated motion

result_quantity.name: The displacement of the car braking for 3 seconds
